medications.indication_rationale: Aggressive fever control is appropriate given fever is the identified arrhythmic trigger in this presentation; the patient has already taken a dose. Continued antipyretic use may be reasonable while awaiting emergency transfer, but this must never be positioned as an alternative to emergency escalation — it is adjunctive only.
medications.dose_route_frequency: as previously taken by patient; further dosing per paramedic/ED clinical assessment
contraindications_in_this_case: None identified in this case, but definitive fever management and any further dosing should be directed by the attending paramedics/ED team, not decided solely via telehealth

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Watchful waiting / 'sleep it off' at home
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Advising the patient to simply go back to sleep and monitor at home is contraindicated given the strong red-flag constellation (agonal breathing, family history of sudden death, fever trigger). The harm is the delay in accessing monitored cardiac care during a period of ongoing arrhythmic risk.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do not go back to sleep or stay home unmonitored following this event. Remain awake and with the partner present until emergency services arrive.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The patient remains at risk of a recurrent, potentially non-self-terminating arrhythmic event while febrile; having someone present and awake maximises the chance of early recognition and CPR if a further event occurs before help arrives.

patient_education_points.point: What happened overnight — passing out with unusual breathing — can be a sign of a serious heart rhythm problem, even though your heart has always seemed fine before and you feel okay right now. Some heart rhythm conditions don't cause any symptoms until an event like this happens.
Please call 000 for an ambulance now. This needs to be checked with heart monitoring and an ECG in hospital, not managed at home overnight.

Your brother's sudden death at a young age is really important information for your own health and possibly for other family members too — please make sure you mention this clearly to the paramedics and hospital team.
Keep treating the fever with paracetamol as directed, but this is in addition to — not instead of — going to hospital.
Your partner should stay with you and be ready to call 000 again and start CPR if you become unresponsive and are not breathing normally before the ambulance arrives.

follow_up_plan.interval: Superseded by emergency escalation — inpatient cardiology/electrophysiology follow-up to be arranged by the treating hospital team
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Post-discharge: confirmation or exclusion of Brugada syndrome/inherited channelopathy via ECG and specialist work-up, consideration of implantable cardioverter-defibrillator if confirmed, genetic counselling and cascade screening for first-degree relatives given the family history of sudden death
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any recurrence of collapse, abnormal breathing, chest discomfort, or palpitations before the ambulance arrives — call 000 immediately and do not wait.

must_include_items: Call 000 for an ambulance immediately — do not allow the patient to go back to sleep or wait until morning
Explicit statement that this may represent a serious heart rhythm problem, not just a faint or a fever
Instruction for partner to remain present, monitor the patient, and be ready to perform CPR if the patient becomes unresponsive and stops breathing normally before paramedics arrive
Recognition that family history of sudden unexplained death at a young age is a major red flag requiring urgent cardiac assessment
acceptable_alternatives: Continuing paracetamol for fever control alongside — not instead of — emergency transfer is acceptable
'Probable serious heart rhythm problem', 'possible inherited heart condition', or 'cannot rule out a dangerous arrhythmia' are all acceptable framings of the cardiac concern
errors_of_omission: No emergency escalation recommended — patient not directed to call 000
Family history of sudden death not asked about or not acted upon
Fever not identified as a possible arrhythmic trigger
No safety-netting instruction for the partner regarding CPR readiness
errors_of_commission: Reassuring the patient that this was 'just a faint' or 'just the fever' and advising them to go back to sleep
Treating the fever as an isolated viral illness requiring only symptomatic home management
Scheduling routine or non-urgent GP follow-up as the primary management action
Advising the patient to self-transport rather than call an ambulance
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: CRITICAL CASE: Any response that does not include both (1) emergency ambulance call (000) AND (2) explicit recognition that this may be a serious cardiac rhythm problem (not simply a faint or fever) is an automatic FAIL on the management domain, regardless of other management quality. This is a T5 case — the minimum acceptable output is emergency escalation with CPR safety-netting for the partner.
